question:
Given that $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\} = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, find,
with proof, the largest possible value, as a function of $n$ (with $n
\geq 2$), of
\[
x_1x_2 + x_2x_3 + \cdots + x_{n-1}x_n + x_nx_1.
\]
solution:
View $x_1, \dots, x_n$ as an arrangement of the numbers $1, 2, \dots,
n$ on a circle.
We prove that the optimal arrangement is
\[
\dots, n-4, n-2, n, n-1, n-3, \dots
\]
To show this, note that if
$a, b$ is a pair of adjacent numbers and $c,d$ is another pair (read
in the same order around the circle) with $a < d$ and $b > c$, then
the segment from $b$ to $c$ can be reversed, increasing the sum by
\[
ac + bd - ab - cd = (d-a)(b-c) > 0.
\]
Now relabel the numbers so they appear in order as follows:
\[
\dots, a_{n-4}, a_{n-2}, a_n = n, a_{n-1}, a_{n-3}, \dots
\]
where without loss of generality we assume $a_{n-1} > a_{n-2}$. By
considering the pairs $a_{n-2}, a_n$ and $a_{n-1}, a_{n-3}$ and using
the trivial fact $a_n > a_{n-1}$, we deduce $a_{n-2} > a_{n-3}$. We
then compare the pairs $a_{n-4}, a_{n-2}$ and $a_{n-1}, a_{n-3}$, and
using that $a_{n-1} > a_{n-2}$, we deduce $a_{n-3} > a_{n-4}$.
Continuing in this
fashion, we prove that $a_n > a_{n-1} > \dots > a_1$ and
so $a_k = k$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$, i.e.\ that the optimal
arrangement is as claimed. In particular, the maximum value of the sum
is
\begin{multline*}
1 \cdot 2 + (n-1)\cdot n + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (n-2)\cdot n \\
\begin{aligned}
&= 2 + n^2 - n + (1^2 - 1) + \cdots + [(n-1)^2 - 1] \\
&= n^2 - n + 2 - (n-1) + \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} \\
&= \frac{2n^3 + 3n^2 - 11n + 18}{6}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

Alternate solution: We prove by induction that the value given above
is an upper bound; it is clearly a lower bound because of the
arrangement given above. Assume this is the case for $n-1$. The optimal
arrangement for $n$ is obtained from some arrangement for $n-1$ by
inserting $n$ between some pair $x, y$ of adjacent terms. This
operation increases the sum by $nx + ny - xy = n^2 - (n-x)(n-y)$,
which is an increasing function of both $x$ and $y$. In particular,
this difference is maximal when $x$ and $y$ equal $n-1$ and $n-2$.
Fortunately, this yields precisely the difference between the claimed
upper bound for $n$ and the assumed upper bound for $n-1$, completing
the induction.

descriptive_long.question:
Given that $\{firstmember, secondmember, \ldots, terminalmember\} = \{1, 2, \ldots, totalcount\}$, find,
with proof, the largest possible value, as a function of $totalcount$ (with $totalcount \geq 2$), of
\[
firstmember\cdot secondmember + secondmember\cdot x_3 + \cdots + penultmember\cdot terminalmember + terminalmember\cdot firstmember.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
View firstmember, \dots, terminalmember as an arrangement of the numbers $1, 2, \dots, totalcount$ on a circle.
We prove that the optimal arrangement is
\[
\dots, totalcount-4, totalcount-2, totalcount, totalcount-1, totalcount-3, \dots
\]
To show this, note that if genericadjacent, secondadjacent is a pair of adjacent numbers and thirdadjacent, fourthadjacent is another pair (read in the same order around the circle) with genericadjacent < fourthadjacent and secondadjacent > thirdadjacent, then the segment from secondadjacent to thirdadjacent can be reversed, increasing the sum by
\[
genericadjacent\cdot thirdadjacent + secondadjacent\cdot fourthadjacent - genericadjacent\cdot secondadjacent - thirdadjacent\cdot fourthadjacent = (fourthadjacent-genericadjacent)(secondadjacent-thirdadjacent) > 0.
\]
Now relabel the numbers so they appear in order as follows:
\[
\dots, labelnminusfour, labelnminustwo, labeln = totalcount, labelnminusone, labelnminusthree, \dots
\]
where without loss of generality we assume labelnminusone > labelnminustwo. By considering the pairs labelnminustwo, labeln and labelnminusone, labelnminusthree and using the trivial fact labeln > labelnminusone, we deduce labelnminustwo > labelnminusthree. We then compare the pairs labelnminusfour, labelnminustwo and labelnminusone, labelnminusthree, and using that labelnminusone > labelnminustwo, we deduce labelnminusthree > labelnminusfour.
Continuing in this fashion, we prove that labeln > labelnminusone > \dots > labelone and so labelk = iterateindex for iterateindex = 1, 2, \dots, totalcount, i.e.\ that the optimal arrangement is as claimed. In particular, the maximum value of the sum is
\begin{multline*}
1 \cdot 2 + (totalcount-1)\cdot totalcount + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (totalcount-2)\cdot totalcount \\
\begin{aligned}
&= 2 + totalcount^2 - totalcount + (1^2 - 1) + \cdots + [(totalcount-1)^2 - 1] \\
&= totalcount^2 - totalcount + 2 - (totalcount-1) + \frac{(totalcount-1) totalcount(2 totalcount-1)}{6} \\
&= \frac{2 totalcount^3 + 3 totalcount^2 - 11 totalcount + 18}{6}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

Alternate solution: We prove by induction that the value given above is an upper bound; it is clearly a lower bound because of the arrangement given above. Assume this is the case for totalcount-1. The optimal arrangement for totalcount is obtained from some arrangement for totalcount-1 by inserting totalcount between some pair insertleft, insertright of adjacent terms. This operation increases the sum by $totalcount\cdot insertleft + totalcount\cdot insertright - insertleft\cdot insertright = totalcount^2 - (totalcount-insertleft)(totalcount-insertright)$, which is an increasing function of both insertleft and insertright. In particular, this difference is maximal when insertleft and insertright equal totalcount-1 and totalcount-2. Fortunately, this yields precisely the difference between the claimed upper bound for totalcount and the assumed upper bound for totalcount-1, completing the induction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Given that $\{pineapple, caterpillar, \ldots, marshmallow\} = \{1, 2, \ldots, constellation\}$, find,
with proof, the largest possible value, as a function of constellation (with constellation
\geq 2), of
\[
pineapplecaterpillar + caterpillar x_3 + \cdots + waterfowlermarshmallow + marshmallowpineapple.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
View pineapple, \dots, marshmallow as an arrangement of the numbers $1, 2, \dots,
constellation$ on a circle.
We prove that the optimal arrangement is
\[
\dots, constellation-4, constellation-2, constellation, constellation-1, constellation-3, \dots
\]
To show this, note that if
buttercup, sailcloth is a pair of adjacent numbers and stargazer,moonstone is another pair (read
in the same order around the circle) with buttercup < moonstone and sailcloth > stargazer, then
the segment from sailcloth to stargazer can be reversed, increasing the sum by
\[
buttercup stargazer + sailcloth moonstone - buttercup sailcloth - stargazer moonstone = (moonstone-buttercup)(sailcloth-stargazer) > 0.
\]
Now relabel the numbers so they appear in order as follows:
\[
\dots, raincloud, honeysuckle, blackbird = constellation, huckleberry, thunderbolt, \dots
\]
where without loss of generality we assume huckleberry > honeysuckle. By
considering the pairs honeysuckle, blackbird and huckleberry, thunderbolt and using
the trivial fact blackbird > huckleberry, we deduce honeysuckle > thunderbolt. We
then compare the pairs raincloud, honeysuckle and huckleberry, thunderbolt, and
using that huckleberry > honeysuckle, we deduce thunderbolt > raincloud.
Continuing in this
fashion, we prove that blackbird > huckleberry > \dots > silhouette and
so windchime = whirlpool for whirlpool = 1, 2, \dots, constellation, i.e.\ that the optimal
arrangement is as claimed. In particular, the maximum value of the sum
is
\begin{multline*}
1 \cdot 2 + (constellation-1)\cdot constellation + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (constellation-2)\cdot constellation \\
\begin{aligned}
&= 2 + constellation^2 - constellation + (1^2 - 1) + \cdots + [(constellation-1)^2 - 1] \\
&= constellation^2 - constellation + 2 - (constellation-1) + \frac{(constellation-1)\,constellation(2\,constellation-1)}{6} \\
&= \frac{2\,constellation^3 + 3\,constellation^2 - 11\,constellation + 18}{6}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

Alternate solution: We prove by induction that the value given above
is an upper bound; it is clearly a lower bound because of the
arrangement given above. Assume this is the case for constellation-1. The optimal
arrangement for constellation is obtained from some arrangement for constellation-1 by
inserting constellation between some pair quartzite, lighthouse of adjacent terms. This
operation increases the sum by constellationquartzite + constellationlighthouse - quartzitelighthouse = constellation^2 - (constellation-quartzite)(constellation-lighthouse),
which is an increasing function of both quartzite and lighthouse. In particular,
this difference is maximal when quartzite and lighthouse equal constellation-1 and constellation-2.
Fortunately, this yields precisely the difference between the claimed
upper bound for constellation and the assumed upper bound for constellation-1, completing
the induction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Given that $\{invariantone, invarianttwo, \ldots, invariantn\} = \{1, 2, \ldots, emptysize\}$, find,
with proof, the largest possible value, as a function of $emptysize$ (with $emptysize
\geq 2$), of
\[
invariantone invarianttwo + invarianttwo x_3 + \cdots + invariantnminusone invariantn + invariantn invariantone.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
View $invariantone, \dots, invariantn$ as an arrangement of the numbers $1, 2, \dots,
emptysize$ on a circle.
We prove that the optimal arrangement is
\[
\dots, emptysize-4, emptysize-2, emptysize, emptysize-1, emptysize-3, \dots
\]
To show this, note that if
$fixedvar, rigidbvalue$ is a pair of adjacent numbers and $rigidcvalue, rigiddvalue$ is another pair (read
in the same order around the circle) with $fixedvar < rigiddvalue$ and $rigidbvalue > rigidcvalue$, then
the segment from $rigidbvalue$ to $rigidcvalue$ can be reversed, increasing the sum by
\[
fixedvar rigidcvalue + rigidbvalue rigiddvalue - fixedvar rigidbvalue - rigidcvalue rigiddvalue = (rigiddvalue-fixedvar)(rigidbvalue-rigidcvalue) > 0.
\]
Now relabel the numbers so they appear in order as follows:
\[
\dots, fixednminusfour, fixednminustwo, fixednvalue = emptysize, fixednminusone, fixednminusthree, \dots
\]
where without loss of generality we assume $fixednminusone > fixednminustwo$. By
considering the pairs $fixednminustwo, fixednvalue$ and $fixednminusone, fixednminusthree$ and using
the trivial fact $fixednvalue > fixednminusone$, we deduce $fixednminustwo > fixednminusthree$. We
then compare the pairs $fixednminusfour, fixednminustwo$ and $fixednminusone, fixednminusthree$, and
using that $fixednminusone > fixednminustwo$, we deduce $fixednminusthree > fixednminusfour$.
Continuing in this
fashion, we prove that $fixednvalue > fixednminusone > \dots > fixedone$ and
so $fixedkayvalue = \immutabledigitk$ for $\immutabledigitk = 1, 2, \dots, emptysize$, i.e.\ that the optimal
arrangement is as claimed. In particular, the maximum value of the sum
is
\begin{multline*}
1 \cdot 2 + (emptysize-1)\cdot emptysize + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (emptysize-2)\cdot emptysize \\
\begin{aligned}
&= 2 + emptysize^2 - emptysize + (1^2 - 1) + \cdots + [(emptysize-1)^2 - 1] \\
&= emptysize^2 - emptysize + 2 - (emptysize-1) + \frac{(emptysize-1)emptysize(2emptysize-1)}{6} \\
&= \frac{2emptysize^3 + 3emptysize^2 - 11emptysize + 18}{6}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

Alternate solution: We prove by induction that the value given above
is an upper bound; it is clearly a lower bound because of the
arrangement given above. Assume this is the case for $emptysize-1$. The optimal
arrangement for $emptysize$ is obtained from some arrangement for $emptysize-1$ by
inserting $emptysize$ between some pair $steadyvar, solidvar$ of adjacent terms. This
operation increases the sum by $emptysize steadyvar + emptysize solidvar - steadyvar solidvar = emptysize^2 - (emptysize-steadyvar)(emptysize-solidvar)$,
which is an increasing function of both $steadyvar$ and $solidvar$. In particular,
this difference is maximal when $steadyvar$ and $solidvar$ equal $emptysize-1$ and $emptysize-2$.
Fortunately, this yields precisely the difference between the claimed
upper bound for $emptysize$ and the assumed upper bound for $emptysize-1$, completing
the induction.

garbled_string.question:
Given that $\{qzxwvtnp, plkokymn, \ldots, djxvhaet\} = \{1, 2, \ldots, kqzmrfla\}$, find,
with proof, the largest possible value, as a function of $kqzmrfla$ (with $kqzmrfla
\geq 2$), of
\[
qzxwvtnp\,plkokymn + plkokymn x_3 + \cdots + ksuhbrpq\,djxvhaet + djxvhaet\,qzxwvtnp.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
View $qzxwvtnp, \dots, djxvhaet$ as an arrangement of the numbers $1, 2, \dots,
kqzmrfla$ on a circle.
We prove that the optimal arrangement is
\[
\dots, kqzmrfla-4, kqzmrfla-2, kqzmrfla, kqzmrfla-1, kqzmrfla-3, \dots
\]
To show this, note that if
$govidhqx, cxdetuom$ is a pair of adjacent numbers and $fpzelkrs,hqtvjysw$ is another pair (read
in the same order around the circle) with $govidhqx < hqtvjysw$ and $cxdetuom > fpzelkrs$, then
the segment from $cxdetuom$ to $fpzelkrs$ can be reversed, increasing the sum by
\[
govidhqx\,fpzelkrs + cxdetuom\,hqtvjysw - govidhqx\,cxdetuom - fpzelkrs\,hqtvjysw = (hqtvjysw-govidhqx)(cxdetuom-fpzelkrs) > 0.
\]
Now relabel the numbers so they appear in order as follows:
\[
\dots, vtmpazqc, dmxosfei, yhrklbcp = kqzmrfla, zqtewfon, sbrunhiy, \dots
\]
where without loss of generality we assume $zqtewfon > dmxosfei$. By
considering the pairs $dmxosfei, yhrklbcp$ and $zqtewfon, sbrunhiy$ and using
the trivial fact $yhrklbcp > zqtewfon$, we deduce $dmxosfei > sbrunhiy$. We
then compare the pairs $vtmpazqc, dmxosfei$ and $zqtewfon, sbrunhiy$, and
using that $zqtewfon > dmxosfei$, we deduce $sbrunhiy > vtmpazqc$.
Continuing in this
fashion, we prove that $yhrklbcp > zqtewfon > \dots > knecaotd$ and
so $wnvlsjqg = rbnqlyxp$ for $rbnqlyxp = 1, 2, \dots, kqzmrfla$, i.e.\ that the optimal
arrangement is as claimed. In particular, the maximum value of the sum
is
\begin{multline*}
1 \cdot 2 + (kqzmrfla-1)\cdot kqzmrfla + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + \cdots + (kqzmrfla-2)\cdot kqzmrfla \\
\begin{aligned}
&= 2 + kqzmrfla^2 - kqzmrfla + (1^2 - 1) + \cdots + [(kqzmrfla-1)^2 - 1] \\
&= kqzmrfla^2 - kqzmrfla + 2 - (kqzmrfla-1) + \frac{(kqzmrfla-1)kqzmrfla(2kqzmrfla-1)}{6} \\
&= \frac{2kqzmrfla^3 + 3kqzmrfla^2 - 11kqzmrfla + 18}{6}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

Alternate solution: We prove by induction that the value given above
is an upper bound; it is clearly a lower bound because of the
arrangement given above. Assume this is the case for $kqzmrfla-1$. The optimal
arrangement for $kqzmrfla$ is obtained from some arrangement for $kqzmrfla-1$ by
inserting $kqzmrfla$ between some pair $lwzbkqve, ujacrnfs$ of adjacent terms. This
operation increases the sum by $kqzmrfla\,lwzbkqve + kqzmrfla\,ujacrnfs - lwzbkqve\,ujacrnfs = kqzmrfla^2 - (kqzmrfla-lwzbkqve)(kqzmrfla-ujacrnfs)$,
which is an increasing function of both $lwzbkqve$ and $ujacrnfs$. In particular,
this difference is maximal when $lwzbkqve$ and $ujacrnfs$ equal $kqzmrfla-1$ and $kqzmrfla-2$.
Fortunately, this yields precisely the difference between the claimed
upper bound for $kqzmrfla$ and the assumed upper bound for $kqzmrfla-1$, completing
the induction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \ge 2 be an integer. Arrange the n odd positive integers

1,\,3,\,5,\ldots ,\,2n-1

around a circle in some order and denote the consecutive entries by
x_1,x_2,\dots ,x_n (indices are read modulo n, so x_{n+1}=x_1).
Determine, as an explicit function of n, the largest possible value of the sum

S_n\;=\;x_1x_2+x_2x_3+\dots +x_{n-1}x_n+x_nx_1 .
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix an integer n \ge 2 and read all indices modulo n.
We proceed in five steps.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  A local-improvement lemma
--------------------------------------------------
Let (a,b) and (c,d) be two consecutive ordered pairs that occur in this
order as we walk counter-clockwise around the circle:

\[ \dots ,a,\,b,\,\dots ,c,\,d,\dots \]

If a<d and b>c, then reversing the circular arc from b to c replaces
the two products ab+cd by ac+bd.  The gain is

(ac+bd)-(ab+cd)=(d-a)(b-c)>0.

Hence any arrangement that maximises S_n must satisfy the following
necessary condition.

(*)  Whenever we meet two adjacent pairs (a,b),(c,d) in this order, we
     cannot have a<d and b>c simultaneously.

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Constructing a candidate arrangement
--------------------------------------------------
We now build inductively an arrangement P_n of the numbers
1,3,\dots ,2n-1.
*  Base n=2: the only circle is (1,3), giving S_2=6.
*  Inductive step: assume P_k (k\ge 2) is known.  To obtain P_{k+1},
   insert the new largest number 2(k+1)-1 between the two currently
   largest entries 2k-1 and 2k-3.  (If they appear as 2k-3,2k-1 we
   again insert between them.)

Unwinding the construction one obtains the explicit description

P_n : 1,3,7,11,\dots ,(4\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1),\;\;(4\lfloor n/2\rfloor-3),\dots ,5,

i.e. the numbers that are \equiv 3 (mod 4) appear first in increasing
order, followed by those \equiv 1 (mod 4) in decreasing order.

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Computing S_n for P_n
--------------------------------------------------
Write n=2m or n=2m+1.

(i)  n=2m (even).  The circle reads
1,3,7,\dots ,4m-1,\;4m-3,4m-7,\dots ,5.
The successive products are

1\cdot 3,
(4i-1)(4i+3)   for 1\le i\le m-1,
(4m-1)(4m-3),
(4j+1)(4j-3)   for 1\le j\le m-1.

A direct summation yields

S_{2m}(P_{2m}) = \frac{4(2m)^3-25(2m)+36}{3}=\frac{4n^3-25n+36}{3}.

(ii)  n=2m+1 (odd).  Compared with the previous case one more product
(4m-1)(4m+1)=16m^2-1 appears, while the last sum extends to j=m.  The
result is once more

S_{2m+1}(P_{2m+1}) = \frac{4(2m+1)^3-25(2m+1)+36}{3}
                  = \frac{4n^3-25n+36}{3}.

Thus

(1)  S_n(P_n)=M_n:=\dfrac{4n^3-25n+36}{3}\qquad(\forall\,n\ge 2).

--------------------------------------------------
4.  A matching upper bound (induction on n)
--------------------------------------------------
We prove that every arrangement satisfies S_n\le M_n, with equality
only for P_n (up to rotation and reflection).

Base n=2 is immediate.  Assume the claim for n-1 (n\ge 3) and consider
an optimal arrangement of the n numbers.  Let

L=2n-1   (the largest label),   and   x,y   its two neighbours.

Delete L together with the two products involving it; the remaining
n-1 labelled circle contributes some value T, so

(2)      S_n = T + L(x+y) - xy.

By the induction hypothesis T\le M_{n-1}.  Define

f(x,y):=L(x+y)-xy.

Because f is symmetric and strictly increasing in each variable, its
maximum over the set {1,3,\dots ,L-2} occurs at x=L-2, y=L-4, giving

f_{\max}=L[(L-2)+(L-4)]-(L-2)(L-4)=4n^2-4n-7.

But

M_n-M_{n-1}=4n^2-4n-7=f_{\max},

hence (2) implies S_n\le M_n.  Equality can hold only if
(a) T=M_{n-1}, i.e. the reduced circle is optimal for n-1 numbers, and
(b) {x,y}={L-2,L-4}.

--------------------------------------------------
5.  Completing the induction - existence and uniqueness of P_n
--------------------------------------------------
By the induction hypothesis the reduced circle equals P_{n-1}
(up to rotation/reflection).  In P_{n-1} the two largest entries are
L-2 and L-4 and they are adjacent, so inserting L between them yields
exactly the construction of Section 2, i.e. P_n.
Conversely, inserting L elsewhere---or keeping either neighbour smaller
than L-4---would strictly decrease f(x,y) and hence S_n.
Thus every optimal arrangement is obtained from P_{n-1} in the
prescribed way, proving that, modulo a global reversal of the circle,
P_n is the unique maximiser.

--------------------------------------------------
6.  Final result
--------------------------------------------------
For every integer n \ge 2 the maximum value of

x_1x_2+x_2x_3+\dots +x_{n-1}x_n+x_nx_1

over all circular permutations of the odd numbers 1,3,\dots ,2n-1 is

S_n^{\max}=\boxed{\dfrac{4n^3-25n+36}{3}}.

Divisibility by 3 is guaranteed because
4\equiv 1\; (\mathrm{mod}\,3) and 25\equiv 1\; (\mathrm{mod}\,3), so

4n^3-25n+36 \equiv n^3-n \equiv n(n-1)(n+1) \equiv 0 \pmod 3,

as the product of three consecutive integers is always divisible by
3.
The extremal arrangement is the (essentially unique) P_n described in
Section 2.

core_steps: Place the permutation on a circle (cyclic adjacency).
If two consecutive pairs (a,b) , (c,d) satisfy a<d and b>c, reversing the intermediate arc raises the sum by (d−a)(b−c)>0.
Hence an optimal arrangement cannot contain such ‘crossings’; the labels must occur in one monotone direction around the circle.
Rotate so the largest label comes first; this forces the order n,n−1,…,1 (unique up to rotation).
Evaluate the corresponding cyclic sum and simplify to (2n³+3n²−11n+18)/6.

slot1.description: The concrete values of the labels; only their relative order is used in the argument.

slot2.description: Choice of where to start/which direction we read the circle when naming aₙ=n, aₙ₋₁, … .
